title: Portable Two-Piece Beach Chair
summary: A knock-down beach chair from one cedar fence picket and a couple of furring strips. The seat slides through the back to lock together — no hardware, no hinges.
description:
This is the classic two-piece beach chair: the seat frame slides through the back frame, the two lean against each other, and your own weight locks the joint. No hinges, no bolts, nothing to rust shut. Pull the seat out and the two flat pieces stack in a trunk or hang on a garage wall.

The materials are as cheap as woodworking gets — one 6-foot cedar fence picket does all the visible slats, and the frame is furring strips. The plan comes in two weights: a 2x2 frame that's sturdier and takes screws without complaint, and a 1x2 frame that's noticeably lighter to carry across sand but must be pre-drilled everywhere, because a screw driven straight into dry 1x2 furring will split it more often than not.

The one dimension that decides whether this chair works is the fit between the seat frame and the back frame. Too tight and it jams when the wood swells at the beach; too loose and the chair racks side to side. Build the back first, then fit the seat to it with about 1/8" of play per side — fit, not measure, because your fence picket widths will vary.

tools.note: Everything is a straight crosscut. A miter saw is quicker for the batch of picket cuts.
And pre-drill EVERYTHING if you build the lightweight 1x2 version — furring strips split.
Pilot holes near board ends, mandatory on the 1x2 version.
Fence pickets arrive rough-sawn. Coarse-grit the whole picket before cutting — bare skin sits on this.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72"
materials.note: Not dog-eared. All ten slats come out of this one picket — sand it with coarse paper before cutting.

materials.name: 2x2 furring strip, 8 ft
materials.note: The stronger-version frame. For the lightweight version substitute ~12 linear feet of 1x2 and shorter screws.

materials.name: 1x3 furring strip
materials.note: Back frame supports — 30" total, scrap works.

materials.name: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-3/4", self-tapping, star drive
materials.note: Use 1-1/4" if building the 1x2 lightweight version.

materials.name: Wood glue
materials.note: Exterior-rated — this lives outside and gets wet.

cutList.note: Pickets are nominally 5/8" thick — some run 1/2". Three slats go on the back frame, two on the seat.
11" long if you build the 1x2 lightweight version — the thinner legs change the inside width.

steps.title: Lay out and tack the back frame
steps.body:
Lay the two 30" back legs on a flat surface, mark the bottom support location, and attach the first slat and supports with glue and ONE screw per corner.

Work fast here — the single screws are pivots for squaring in the next step, and you want to be done adjusting before the glue tacks up.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-3/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Square the frame, then finish screwing
steps.body:
Measure both diagonals of the back frame. If one is longer, push those two corners toward each other and re-measure until the diagonals match — that's what square means, no protractor required.

Then add the remaining screws. If you're building the 1x2 lightweight version, pre-drill every one of them; a split leg at this stage means starting over.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-3/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Slat the back
steps.body: Screw the remaining back slats to the frame, keeping the gaps even. Even gaps are the difference between 'handmade' and 'homemade' on a piece this simple.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-3/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Add the back support
steps.body: Flip the assembly and attach the 1x3 back support across the rear face. This closes out the back frame — the slot the seat slides through now exists.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-3/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Fit the seat legs through the back frame
steps.body:
Slide the two 26" seat legs through the back frame opening. This is a FIT step, not a measure step: you want roughly 1/8" of play beside each leg. Too snug? Rotate the 2x2s a quarter turn (they're rarely perfectly square), or round the corners and sand until they slide.

Remember cedar swells when damp — a fit that's snug in the garage is stuck at the beach.

steps.title: Slat the seat
steps.body: With the seat legs still sitting in the back frame at their working angle, lay the remaining fence picket slats on them, adjust the positions, and screw them down. Fitting the slats in-place like this guarantees the assembled chair's geometry — building the seat flat on a bench does not.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-3/4", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Add the seat support and test
steps.body:
Pull the seat frame out and screw the short support between the legs on the inside — 8" for the 2x2 build, 11" for the 1x2 build.

Reassemble and sit down, gently the first time. The joint tightens under load; if anything creaks, find it now on the driveway rather than later on the sand.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-3/4", self-tapping, star drive

images.alt: Two-piece wooden beach chair with cedar slats, disassembled into flat seat and back sections
